title: Roast Carrots
subtitle: Whole carrots with butter and thyme, caramelised in a hot oven
excerpt: Whole roast carrots take longer than most people expect: 40 to 50 minutes at 200°C for a large carrot to become tender all the way through and caramelised on the outside. Butter, a little honey, and thyme are the classic companions. Roast them separately from the main joint so they can have a higher oven position where the heat is more direct.
sourceNotes: Traditional British Sunday roast accompaniment. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: glazed carrots as a side dish. Classic British home cooking method.

content.text: Roast carrots are one of the easier accompaniments on the Sunday table but one that is often undercooked. A large carrot, left whole or halved lengthways, needs a full 40 to 50 minutes at 200°C to soften all the way through. Rushing the time gives a carrot that is charred on the outside and hard at the centre.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Toss the halved {{carrot}} carrots in a mix of {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter and {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Lay cut-side down in a single layer in the roasting pan with the thyme sprigs. Roast for 35 minutes without disturbing.

Turn the carrots cut-side up. Drizzle with {{honey}} of honey if using. Return to the oven for a further 10 minutes until the cut faces are caramelised and the carrots are completely tender when pierced with a knife tip.

rows.fix: Drop to 180°C (160°C fan) and cover with foil for 15 minutes to steam the interior through.
rows.cause: Oven too hot for the size of carrot, or carrots were very thick
rows.symptom: Carrots charred on the outside but still hard inside

rows.fix: Use a rack of butter rather than a pool: just enough to coat, not to pool. A very lightly oiled pan helps the cut face make direct contact.
rows.cause: Carrots sitting in butter pool rather than on a dry pan surface
rows.symptom: No colour developing on the cut faces

content.text: Roast carrots are one of the quieter presences on the Sunday table: they do not assert themselves the way a Yorkshire pudding or a good gravy does, but a lunch without them feels incomplete. Their sweetness is a useful counterpoint to the richness of beef or pork.
